title: ECCG April 2026 — Event-Based SLAM & Reconstruction
transcript: Guillermo Gallego: Welcome everyone to the April 2026 session of the Event Camera Community Group. We have a packed agenda today, kicking off with reconstruction and SLAM. Davide, do you want to introduce the first paper? Davide Scaramuzza: Sure. The headline preprint this month is EventSLAM-GS: Event-Based SLAM with 3D Gaussian Splatting, which I think is genuinely a step change. It builds a dense 3D Gaussian-splatting map online from a single DVS camera and matches ORB-SLAM3 within six percent RMSE without intensity frames. The code is up on GitHub, so people can play with it today. Daniel Cremers: We had a few of our students try EventSLAM-GS this week. The event-rate-adaptive tracking is the key trick — it's what lets the renderer stay differentiable under heavy motion. We want to compare it directly with the Rebecq E2VID++ paper. Guillermo Gallego: Speaking of which, E2VID++ from Henri Rebecq and Daniel Gehrig hit CVPR. Higher-Resolution Event-to-Video Reconstruction with Transformers. It pushes PSNR by 1.8 dB on ECD and MVSEC. Tobi Delbruck: A practical observation: the streaming attention module in E2VID++ is the unlock for real-time 720p. We've integrated it into the iniVation toolchain locally. Chiara Bartolozzi: I want to flag the TPAMI survey, A Decade of Event-Based Vision: A Survey of 1200 Papers. Gallego, Delbruck, and Orchard organised the field across 14 open problems. It is the new reference. We should make sure everyone here cites it consistently. Davide Scaramuzza: Agreed. There's also the DSEC-Flow dataset paper out of my group — DSEC-Flow: A Large-Scale Driving Dataset for Event-Based Optical Flow. Twelve hours of synced data, four times the size of MVSEC. The dataset includes night, tunnel, and rain, and the night split is especially valuable because performance degrades 32 percent there. Daniel Cremers: A community ask: can we standardise the splits so cross-paper comparisons are not apples to oranges? Davide Scaramuzza: We released the official splits with the paper, but I take the point — let's add it to the action items. Tobi Delbruck: On the hardware side: the Nature Electronics piece, Towards a Tier-1 Spike Camera, hit the news. Six and a half megapixels, 100 kfps effective rate, more than 140 dB dynamic range. Guillermo Gallego: Let us also note the Loihi paper, Asynchronous Spiking Object Detection on Loihi 2 for Drone Collision Avoidance, in Science Robotics. End-to-end on a 250-gram quadrotor with eight times lower power than the GPU baseline at equal accuracy. Action items: I'll send a recap email by Friday. Davide, please share the DSEC-Flow split commitment. Chiara, can you lead the working group on the tactile roadmap, Neuromorphic Tactile Sensing: A Roadmap that you preprinted last week? Chiara Bartolozzi: Yes, I will host a focused session next month. Guillermo Gallego: Open questions before we wrap: One, can the spiking-on-Loihi pipeline generalise beyond drone collision? Two, will Gaussian splatting on events scale to dynamic outdoor scenes? Three, do we agree on a community benchmark suite for event-based reconstruction? Let us continue these on the mailing list. Thanks all.

title: ECCG March 2026 — Datasets, Simulators, and Benchmarks
transcript: Mathias Gehrig: Welcome to the March 2026 ECCG session. Today's focus is datasets, simulators, and benchmarks. Quick agenda: v2e-Lite, DSEC-Flow updates, differentiable event generation, and a discussion on community benchmarks. Tobi Delbruck: I'll start. We've been working on v2e-Lite: Energy-Efficient Frame-to-Event Conversion for Edge Deployments. The big number is 540 frames per second on a Jetson Orin Nano while preserving 96 percent of the event-Jaccard fidelity of the original v2e. We're using it as a pretraining bootstrap for several downstream tasks. Yuhuang Hu: We're shipping v2e-Lite as a tool in the iniVation SDK starting next quarter. Engineers can drop a video into the pipeline and get reasonable training events without a real sensor. Cornelia Fermüller: That's huge for academic labs without sensor access. How well does it transfer to real DVS data? Tobi Delbruck: We see roughly 92 percent of the gain when we then fine-tune on a small real dataset. So it's a bootstrap, not a replacement. Damien Joubert: Related work: Differentiable Event Generation for End-to-End Closed-Loop Training, presented at WACV. The contribution is making v2e itself differentiable. It enables closed-loop optical-flow training and shows a 1.4 EPE improvement over teacher-student baselines. Mathias Gehrig: On real data: DSEC-Flow is now public. Twelve hours, four times MVSEC, day/night/tunnel/rain. We benchmarked seven methods and night performance drops 32 percent. The eval server is open. Cornelia Fermüller: Strong support for a standardised reconstruction benchmark, similar to what KITTI did for stereo. We could anchor it on DSEC-Flow and MVSEC. Tobi Delbruck: Agreed. The 2026 TPAMI survey, A Decade of Event-Based Vision, calls out exactly this gap. Mathias Gehrig: Action items: I'll draft a benchmark proposal by end of month. Yuhuang, please send the v2e-Lite SDK release notes when ready. Damien, can you publish the differentiable v2e weights on a public mirror? Damien Joubert: Yes, will do this week. Mathias Gehrig: Open questions: One, what's the right metric beyond EPE for event-based flow? Two, can we incentivise the community to actually submit to a shared leaderboard rather than publish on private splits? See you all in April.
